author: @justinkemeter
text: Ok to challenge the argument, this is a motor generator set and not a dynamotor as they don’t share a rotor and are just a motor and generator coupled by a shaft

author: @RyanLebeck-td5ft
text:
One big advantage is that they both have the same specifications, so it IS a direct conversion.

Much like how you bang on about a motor generates a certain output of rpm, a matched motor converted or operating as a generator should take that rpm provided by the paired motor and directly convert it into a commensurate current output.

Proof is in the pudding they say eh mate?

author: @annoloki
text: You don't need the second fan, just put the 12V DC into the first, and you're getting the switched current from the wires you've soldered onto the output from the hall effect sensor. It's not three-phase, one is a common center tap, and the other two are the ends of the coil, which you can take your AC from. You still need the spinning magnet, as that is what provides the timing to the hall effect sensor, telling it which end of the coil to apply current to. Often, the components will take a higher voltage, like, 18V, but the current can't go too high or they'll overheat.

author: @hackmaster124
text: So my house is powered by a very good 5kw solid state inverter. If I wanted to go mechanical I would need two 5kw motors plus a beefy transformer. That stuff alone would be more than twice the cost of the solid state inverter and I'm sure it would be more than twice as loud at full load. Not that I don't think this is cool idea but it's just not practical for large power generation. Maybe this can be scaled up and combined with a flywheel for both mechanical energy storage and inversion.

author: @CitizenSmith50
text: can you not go back to the the very early principal of the electro-mechanical  trembler coill as first used on the very early Benz and Ford motors before the introduction of contact breakers on the distributor? The trembler coil was a device called a Ruhmkorff or induction coil and used an electromagnetic breaker on the same principal of early electric bells.

You can adjust the gap on the points of the coil to get the proper frequency and use a capacitor to reduce arcing at the points.

This really has potential, I used to work on the old Rediline inverters in the 80s & 90s, but they had brushes but they were easy to change. The brushless idea is much better, you would need a large fan maybe to scale it up to a 3000watt inverter.
I suppose to get the voltage you want, you could either bung the AC output into a transformer, or simply use a mains fan on one side and a twelve volt on the other.
I certainly have a few applications for this idea, thank you for the inspiration.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Nice. But I think you realise the control electronics in the driving part is pretty much a small inverter in itself. A simple version, but that's what it is.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - I do realise that - but thanks for pointing it out

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes. Inverters don't need to be very complicated. But current control, short proof, high voltage range, All things you will need for example in a car make it complicated and expensive. It can be fun to look up the datasheet of the IC used in the fan control electronics and see what features it has.

author: @strongforce8466
text:
Thats impressive, I like how  you look at old designs and come up with gems like that, so the motor you use here was the fan motor? brilliant..

A nice follow up video would be how to make a circuit to add those in series to create like 120v or 220v AC , I'm a bit lost but I think with DC its just like batteries you can add up the voltage in series, not sure about the AC mmh.

Athough might be a bit complicated and also dangerous levels of voltage so maybe its not a good idea mmh but people building such stuff as an inverter should know the danger and also I guess the cables might need to be rated for 220v aswell, anyways very interesting stuff

author: @Kuršiukas
text: So, add some transistors and you have perfect 3 phase gate for DC battery (lets say from photovoltaic or wind turbine) and they should generate decent 3 phase output based on this low amp signal? ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: now then - I like the thinking mate - cheers

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: That was where my mind went. This application is perfect for anything that requires a true sine wave reference. I've designed circuits that use just a quad op amp and a handful of passives to very very closely approximate a sine wave, but it's near impossible to make it perfect, and this does that right off the bat.

author: @watahyahknow
text: couple a thousant pounds for an inverter ? eeeeh nope : the chineese ones are between 20 and 150 quid import and tax fees might be an issue though with the brexit in full effect

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think it depends on the size of inverter mate - Luke's famiiy just got one for their solar installation it was UK£2k - you are quite right a little plug in fo your car will set you back only a few pounds

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: Yes, but those cheap Chinese inverters are going to output a "modified sine wave" full of nasty harmonics and really only good for devices that convert AC to DC anyway. Anything that likes a true sine wave like AC motors is going run hot, inefficient, and will buzz on a "modified sine wave" inverter.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: Basically any coil charged with pulses dc will turn into ac. As it absorbs current to its saturation it will show as a sine wave and as the electro magnetic force collapses it to will be in the form of sinusoidal ! Essentially this is doing the same thing but with 3 coils  and mechanically connected to a dc load ! It would be interesting to see the difference in effeciency between these 2 systems . My guess is 20% less for mechanical version but for simplicity and free parts it can’t be beat. First test to do is see if it can charge a phone . 👍🏻😂

text: I dont quite get why you dont just attach your output wires to the stator coils of the dc powered fan. Just get a cheap UPS from local craigslist/fleamarket/decommisioned IT firms. I myself recently got a 4kVA/3.5kW unit used for 50€ including shipping. Thats a price you just cant beat with any DIY version even if you dont consider the fact that the used unit comes with lots of certificates and is guaranteed to be safe.

author: @scottwojo7881
text: Robert,  how many would it take to arrive at 120v AC to drive power tools from vehicle

replies.author: @headlessxp4948
replies.text:
Lets say you get 1W fans. A drill takes about 500W. Lets say the fans are 70% efficient.
500W / (1W * eta) ~= about 700 fans.
And that doesnt even account for the fact that you would need to physically link the rotors together to synchronise the fans properly. If they arent synchronised, the voltage will randomly glitch between 0 and 120VAC

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: Been there, failed that. The problem is speed: To get a BLDC motor to work as a generator you have to spin it much much faster than a wind turbine. You need to either gear it up, or use a many-poled motor more suited to low rotational speed.

replies.author: @peterfenwick2540
replies.text: @vylbird8014 Good point! When I have some old fans will like to try just for experimenting though. Thank you.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: @peterfenwick2540 I had briefly promising results using a belt drive on a small DC motor, but the gears wore through fast. I think you might have some hope with a stepper motor - they should be able to function as workable generators at a much lower speed, which will make the mechanical side much easier.

replies.author: @tremail564
replies.text: Isn't the frequency connected to the input D.C volt/amp which in terms limits the amount of A.C volt/amps the device is bound with in the sticker limitations pulled off at the start? Would that ,magnet trick work to increase the output of the A.C current.

replies.author: @DimitriPappas
replies.text: @Peter_Schluss-Mit-Lustig Wouldn't the output AC frequency depend on the RPM in this case (each revolution producing a complete wave transient)? If so, what about using a stepper motor or some other fixed-rpm motor on the DC side to determine output frequency? Edit: I'm guessing this means that the output frequency is always too high, because the AC generator needs more than 50-60 RPM to produce any meaningful voltage ?

replies.author: @ScarletFlames1
replies.text:
​@DimitriPappas Why stepper motor? Many of those fans have control circuits that let you regulate the RPM. You'd need to create your own software and calculations so that it would up the power to maintain the RPM even under load but that's not particularly hard to do. You can just monitor the output of the generator and send the signal from that monitor as whether to make it go faster or slower to maintain a perfect sine wave. And before someone says "but what if you overload it?" to which my answer would be "then you should have gotten a bigger one, just like regular inverters have a limit so does a mechanical one".

Also, now I REALLY want to see the efficiency ratings of this thing compared to the average inverter on the market. If the efficiency is over 90% of the average inverter it's worth it merely for the price saving of however much an inverter of the same power rating can cost.
